title: The Playbook for a $100M AI Agency
summary: Nate Herk interviews Devin Kearns on building a high-value AI agency. The discussion covers why most current AI services will not survive 2027, identifies mid-market as the prime opportunity over SMBs or enterprises, outlines eleven monetization methods for AI experts, and emphasizes framework-based positioning over generic vendor services.
writeup: Devin Karns, co-founder and CEO of Custom AI Studio, argues that AI practitioners operating small lifestyle agencies are leaving enormous enterprise value on the table at exactly the moment when a large exit is achievable. The central claim is that the commoditization of development skill (a 67-year-old lawyer now ships a viable vibe-coded app) means the premium on hours-based delivery is collapsing, so the sustainable position is domain expertise and systems, not labor. The conversation traces Custom AI Studio's own arc from $2,500 automations to $400,000-$500,000 annual managed-service contracts, and uses that trajectory to frame why the window for building a $100 million exit is open right now as the early majority of companies finally begins acting on AI.
key_points: The market value of raw development labor is in active freefall: Claude and Claude Code are capable enough that non-technical individuals, including a 67-year-old lawyer cited in the conversation, are shipping production-quality apps, which eliminates the premium previously charged for development hours.

Claude 4.6 (Opus 4.6) and the public release of Claude.ai were identified as the specific inflection points that pushed AI adoption from early adopters into the early majority of companies and executives, creating a large new pool of enterprise clients who need implementation guidance rather than just tooling.

A pure AI readiness consultancy generating $2 million in annual revenue sells for roughly $2 million (1x revenue), but a firm generating $6 million in annual revenue can command a 5x multiple and sell for $30 million, making the revenue scale of the business the dominant lever on exit value.

Custom AI Studio's own revenue history illustrates the trajectory: early-stage $2,500 automations scaled to $400,000-$500,000 projects over the course of a year with ongoing managed-service contracts, primarily in the mid-market segment with some enterprise department-level work.

Org charts across industries are consolidating as AI absorbs the headcount layers beneath directors and executives, and practitioners who continue selling hours-based delivery without repositioning toward domain-specific systems risk being displaced by this same consolidation.
